{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868年}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872年}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934年}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958年}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937年}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979年}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154年}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647年}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868年}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872年}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873年}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877年}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868年}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872年}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934年}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958年}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937年}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979年}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154年}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647年}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868年}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872年}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873年}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877年}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868年}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872年}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934年}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958年}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937年}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979年}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154年}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647年}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868年}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872年}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873年}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877年}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} {{Shinto stub}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Gion Shrines]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868年}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934年}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958年}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937年}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979年}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154年}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647年}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868年}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873年}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877年}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868年}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year }} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958年}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937年}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979年}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year }} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647年}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868年}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year }} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877年}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year }} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year }} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year }} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year }} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979年}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year }} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year }} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year }} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year }} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year }} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year }} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year }} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year }} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year }} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year }} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year }} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year }} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year }} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year }} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year }} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} {{Shinto stub}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year }} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year }} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year }} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year }} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year }} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year }} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year }} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year }} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year }} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year }} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year }} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year }} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year }} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year }} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year }} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year }} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year }} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year }} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year }} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year }} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year }} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} {{Shinto stub}} ==References== {{Reflist}} Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|12=draft}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|12=draft}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|12=draft}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|12=draft}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|12=draft}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|12=draft}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|12=draft}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|12=draft}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|12=draft}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|12=draft}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|12=draft}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|12=draft}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|12=draft}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|12=draft}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|12=draft}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|12=draft}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|12=draft}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|12=draft}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|12=draft}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=draft}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|12=draft}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|12=draft}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|12=draft}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|12=draft}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|12=draft}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|12=draft}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|12=draft}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|12=draft}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|12=draft}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}}
