The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|12=draft}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|12=draft}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|12=draft}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|12=draft}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|12=draft}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|12=draft}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|12=draft}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|12=draft}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|12=draft}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|12=draft}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|12=draft}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|12=draft}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|12=draft}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|12=draft}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|12=draft}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|12=draft}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|12=draft}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|12=draft}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|12=draft}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=draft}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|12=draft}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|12=draft}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|12=draft}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|12=draft}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|12=draft}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|12=draft}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|12=draft}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|12=draft}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|12=draft}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|12=draft}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|12=draft}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|12=draft}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|12=draft}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|12=draft}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|12=draft}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|12=draft}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|12=draft}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|12=draft}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|12=draft}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|12=draft}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|12=draft}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|12=draft}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|12=draft}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=draft}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|12=draft}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|12=draft}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|12=draft}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|12=draft}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|12=draft}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|12=draft}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|12=draft}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|12=draft}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|12=draft}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|12=draft}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|12=draft}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|12=draft}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|12=draft}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|12=draft}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|12=draft}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|12=draft}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|12=draft}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|12=draft}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|12=draft}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|12=draft}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|12=draft}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|12=draft}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|12=draft}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|12=draft}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|12=draft}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|12=draft}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|12=draft}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|12=draft}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|12=draft}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|12=draft}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=draft}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|12=draft}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|12=draft}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|12=draft}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|12=draft}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|12=draft}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|12=draft}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|12=draft}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|12=draft}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|12=draft}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|12=draft}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|12=draft}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|12=draft}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|12=draft}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|12=draft}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|12=draft}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|12=draft}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|12=draft}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|12=draft}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|12=draft}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|12=draft}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|12=draft}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|12=draft}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|12=draft}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|12=draft}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|12=draft}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|12=draft}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|12=draft}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|12=draft}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=draft}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|12=draft}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|12=draft}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|12=draft}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|12=draft}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|12=draft}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|12=draft}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|12=draft}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|12=draft}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|12=draft}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, 北岡神社 |所在地 = 熊本市西区春日1丁目8-16 |位置 = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 健速須盞嗚尊
奇稲田姫命
八柱御子神 |社格 = 県社 |創建 = 934年（承平4年） |本殿 = |別名 = 祇園宮 |例祭 = 8月1日 - 3日 |神事 = }} '''北岡神社'''（きたおかじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[西区 (熊本市)|西区]]春日1丁目に鎮座する[[神社]]である。[[平安時代]]に[[京都]]の[[祇園神社|祇園社]]の分霊を勧請して創建されたと伝わり、熊本市域においては[[藤崎八旛宮]]と並ぶ由緒を持つ古社である。旧[[近代社格制度|社格]]は県社[[鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|鈴木喬]] 『熊本の神社と寺院』 [[熊本日日新聞]]社、1980年、22-23頁。 '''祇園社'''、'''祇園宮'''（ぎおんぐう）と称されていたが、[[1868年]]（[[明治]]元年）に[[神仏混交|神仏混交禁止]]を受けて北岡宮に、さらに[[1872年]]（明治4年）に現在の名称に改められた。東側200メートルほど離れた[[坪井川 (熊本県)|坪井川]]に架かる祇園橋と[[熊本市電]]の[[祇園橋停留場]]に旧社名の名残をとどめている。 == 由緒 == 社伝によれば、[[934年]]（[[承平 (日本)|承平]]4年）、[[藤原保昌]]が[[肥後国|肥後守]]として下向したおり、[[京都]]の祇園社（[[八坂神社]]）の分霊を、[[国府]]（現[[熊本市]][[西区 (熊本市)|西区]][[二本木 (熊本市)|二本木]]周辺）の湯ノ原(現二本木5丁目)に鎮護のため勧請したのが創祀であるという。ただし、藤原保昌は[[958年]]（[[958年|天徳]]2年）の生まれで肥後守の任期もかなり後のことなので、そのまま史実とすることはできない。 はじめ'''祇園宮'''と称した。[[937年]](承平7年)には車屋敷(現二本木2丁目)に遷座され、さらに[[979年]]（[[天元 (日本)|天元]]2年）に朝日山へ遷宮した[[熊本日日新聞]]編纂・発行『熊本県大百科事典』、1982年、194頁。同山は祇園山と呼ばれるようになったが、明治に至り[[花岡山]]に改められた。 創建当初から「宝祚無窮、天下泰平、国家安全、悪魔降伏、西九守護」の勅願社であったが、[[1154年]]([[久寿]]元年)に近衛天皇より「顕神院」の勅額とともに、「日本第弐 西九壹社（日本第二、[[西海道]]九カ国第一の祇園社の意）」の尊称を下賜された。 [[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]を経て[[佐々成政]]領国の頃には衰退していたが、[[加藤清正]]により復興。のち[[熊本藩|熊本藩主]][[細川家]]により[[1647年]]（[[正保]]4年）に現在の北岡の社地に遷座し日本歴史地名大系44『熊本県の地名』[[平凡社]]、1985年、525頁、代々の藩主の厚い崇敬を受けた。 [[1868年]]（[[明治]]元年）、北岡宮と改め、さらに[[1872年]]（明治4年）に北岡神社と改称した。[[1873年]]（明治5年）、県社に列した。また、[[1877年]]（明治10年）の[[西南戦争]]の際には、[[薩摩]]軍の本営が一時境内に置かれた角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、377-378頁。 == 祭神 == * [[素戔嗚尊|健速須盞嗚尊]] * [[奇稲田姫命]] * 八柱御子神（[[天忍穂耳命]]、[[天穂日命]]、[[天津日子根命]]、[[活津日子根命]]、[[熊野久須毘命]]、[[多紀理毘売命]]、[[市杵島比売命]]、[[多岐都比売命]]） == 例大祭 == * 8月1日 - 3日 == 境内社 == * 疫神社 - 祭神・蘇民御霊 * 京国司神社 - 祭神・藤原保昌 * 清原神社 - 祭神・[[清原元輔]] == 御神木 == * '''厄除けの夫婦楠''' - 樹齢約1千年の一対の楠。特に厄除開運、夫婦円満、縁結びの御利益を授かると古くより伝わっている。 == 交通 == * [[熊本空港]]、[[九州自動車道]][[熊本インターチェンジ]]より車で約40分 * [[熊本城]]より車で約10分 * [[熊本駅|JR熊本駅]]より車で約2分、徒歩で約4分 * [[熊本市電幹線|熊本市電]]A系統 [[祇園橋停留場|祇園橋]]電停より徒歩で約4分 * [[九州産交バス]]・[[熊本市交通局|熊本市営バス]]・[[熊本電気鉄道|熊本電鉄バス]]・[[熊本バス]]・[[熊本都市バス]] 「北岡神社前」バス停より徒歩で約2分 == 出典 == == 参考文献 == * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、113頁 * 高木盛義『くまもと史跡散歩』熊本新評社、1982年、174-175頁 * 新熊本市史編纂委員会『新熊本市史、別編第2巻』[[熊本市]]、1996年、325-328頁 == 外部リンク == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ 北岡神社] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine 北岡神社]（オールクマモト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:きたおかしんしや}} [[Category:熊本県の旧県社]] [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:熊本市西区の歴史]] [[category:熊本市西区の建築物]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[北岡神社]] to [[Kitaoka Shrine]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|12=draft}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|12=draft}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|12=draft}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|12=draft}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|12=draft}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|12=draft}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|12=draft}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|12=draft}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|12=draft}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|12=draft}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|12=draft}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|12=draft}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|12=draft}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|12=draft}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|12=draft}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|12=draft}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|12=draft}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|12=draft}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|12=draft}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|12=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|12=draft}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|12=draft}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|12=draft}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|12=draft}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|12=draft}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|12=draft}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|12=draft}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|12=draft}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|12=draft}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|12=draft}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|12=draft}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|12=draft}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|12=draft}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|12=draft}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|qq=draft}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|qq=draft}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|qq=draft}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|qq=draft}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|qq=draft}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|qq=draft}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|qq=draft}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|qq=draft}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|qq=draft}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|qq=draft}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|qq=draft}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|qq=draft}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|qq=draft}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|qq=draft}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|qq=draft}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|qq=draft}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|qq=draft}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|qq=draft}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|qq=draft}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|qq=draft}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Gion Shrines]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Soja shrines]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Kitaoka Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} This means all the kami of ]] [[sn:Kitaoka Shrine]] [[sn:itaoka Shrine]]

The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|qq=draft}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|qq=draft}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|qq=draft}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|qq=draft}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|qq=draft}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|qq=draft}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|qq=draft}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|qq=draft}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|qq=draft}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|qq=draft}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|qq=draft}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|qq=draft}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|qq=draft}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|qq=draft}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|qq=draft}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|qq=draft}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|qq=draft}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|qq=draft}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|qq=draft}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|qq=draft}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} {{Shinto stub}} ==References== {{Reflist}} Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}. from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. ==
