revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|qq=}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern Shrine Ranking System|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/934|qq=}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higo Province|qq=}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/958|qq=}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/937|qq=}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/979|qq=}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1154|qq=}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Konoe|qq=}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikaidō|qq=}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasase Narimasa|qq=}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō Kiyomasa|qq=}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1647|qq=}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Domain|qq=}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1873|qq=}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma Rebellion|qq=}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1877|qq=}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma|qq=}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara Gensuke|qq=}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Expressway|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Castle|qq=}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kumamoto Station|qq=}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Gion Shrines]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Soja shrines]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Kitaoka Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern Shrine Ranking System|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/934|qq=}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higo Province|qq=}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/958|qq=}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/937|qq=}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/979|qq=}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1154|qq=}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Konoe|qq=}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikaidō|qq=}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasase Narimasa|qq=}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō Kiyomasa|qq=}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1647|qq=}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Domain|qq=}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1873|qq=}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma Rebellion|qq=}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1877|qq=}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma|qq=}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara Gensuke|qq=}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Expressway|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Castle|qq=}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kumamoto Station|qq=}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Gion Shrines]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Soja shrines]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[simple:Kitaoka Shrine]] [[en:Kitaoka Shrine]] [[zh:%E5%8C%97%E5%86%88%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[ja:%E5%8C%97%E5%B2%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:itaoka-jinja]] [[ja: located in |ja: located in ]] [[ja:. {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|qq=}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern Shrine Ranking System|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/934|qq=}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higo Province|qq=}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/958|qq=}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/937|qq=}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/979|qq=}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1154|qq=}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Konoe|qq=}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikaidō|qq=}} region). Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1647|qq=}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Domain|qq=}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1873|qq=}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma Rebellion|qq=}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1877|qq=}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma|qq=}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara Gensuke|qq=}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Expressway|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Castle|qq=}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kumamoto Station|qq=}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] → [[Shinbutsu bunri]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|qq=}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern Shrine Ranking System|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/934|qq=}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higo Province|qq=}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/958|qq=}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/937|qq=}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/979|qq=}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1154|qq=}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Konoe|qq=}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikaidō|qq=}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasase Narimasa|qq=}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō Kiyomasa|qq=}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1647|qq=}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Domain|qq=}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1873|qq=}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma Rebellion|qq=}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1877|qq=}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma|qq=}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara Gensuke|qq=}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Expressway|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Castle|qq=}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kumamoto Station|qq=}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|qq=}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern Shrine Ranking System|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/934|qq=}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higo Province|qq=}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/958|qq=}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/937|qq=}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/979|qq=}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1154|qq=}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Konoe|qq=}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikaidō|qq=}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasase Narimasa|qq=}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō Kiyomasa|qq=}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1647|qq=}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Domain|qq=}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1873|qq=}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma Rebellion|qq=}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1877|qq=}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma|qq=}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara Gensuke|qq=}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Expressway|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Castle|qq=}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kumamoto Station|qq=}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|qq=}}. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873rd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1873|qq=}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma|qq=}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|qq=}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern Shrine Ranking System|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/934|qq=}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higo Province|qq=}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/958|qq=}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/937|qq=}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/979|qq=}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1154|qq=}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Konoe|qq=}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikaidō|qq=}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasase Narimasa|qq=}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō Kiyomasa|qq=}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1647|qq=}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Domain|qq=}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1873|qq=}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma Rebellion|qq=}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1877|qq=}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma|qq=}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * Kitaoka Shrine |location = {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto

Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}}. It is said to have been established during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}} by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of {{ill|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|ja|藤崎八旛宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine|qq=}}. Under the old {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Shrine Rank|lt_ja=社格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern Shrine Ranking System|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}}, it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in {{ill|1868|ja|1868年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in {{ill|1872|ja|1872年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in {{ill|934|ja|934年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/934|qq=}} (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ({{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|lt=Higo no Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higo Province|qq=}})—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Ward|ja|西区 (熊本市)|lt=Nishi Ward|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Ward|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in {{ill|958|ja|958年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/958|qq=}} (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In {{ill|937|ja|937年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/937|qq=}} (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in {{ill|979|ja|979年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/979|qq=}} (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In {{ill|1154|ja|1154年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1154|qq=}} (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from {{ill|Emperor Konoe|ja|近衛天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Konoe|qq=}} along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the {{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikaidō|qq=}} region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Sasase Narimasa|ja|佐々成政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasase Narimasa|qq=}}, but it was restored by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō Kiyomasa|qq=}}. Later, in {{ill|1647|ja|1647年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1647|qq=}} (Shōhō 4), the lord of the {{ill|Kumamoto Domain|ja|熊本藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Domain|qq=}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In {{ill|1868|ja|1868年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1868|qq=}} (the first year of the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in {{ill|1872|ja|1872年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1872|qq=}} (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In {{ill|1873|ja|1873年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1873|qq=}} (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma Rebellion|qq=}} in {{ill|1877|ja|1877年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1877|qq=}} (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the {{ill|Satsuma|ja|薩摩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satsuma|qq=}} army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Kiyohara Gensuke|ja|清原元輔|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyohara Gensuke|qq=}} == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|熊本空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}, the {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|ja|九州自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Expressway|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|ja|熊本城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Castle|qq=}}. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{ill|JR Kumamoto Station|ja|熊本駅|lt=JR Kumamoto Station|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kumamoto Station|qq=}}. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City Bus|ja|熊本市交通局|lt=Kumamoto City Bus|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|ja|熊本電気鉄道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
