Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:熊本市の神社
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} {{Shinto stub}} Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} {{Shinto stub}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Sōja shrines}} {{translated page|ja|北岡神社|version=100124484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Gion Shrines]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[simple:Kitaoka Shrine]] [[en:Kitaoka Shrine]] After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|天忍穂耳命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} {{Shinto stub}} Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ({{ill|Amanooshihomimitokami|ja|アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanooshihomimitokami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=}} shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=}} History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏習合}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本市交通局}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 細川氏}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ([[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]], {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アマツヒコネ}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イクツヒコネ}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クマノクスビ}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イチキシマヒメ}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} {{Shinto stub}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Sōja shrines}} {{translated page|ja|北岡神社|version=100124484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Gion Shrines]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[simple:Kitaoka Shrine]] [[zh:%E5%8C%97%E5%86%88%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[ja:%E5%8C%97%E5%B2%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:itaoka-jinja]] [[ja: located in |ja: located in ]] [[ja:. This means all the kami of ]] [[sn:Kitaoka Shrine]] [[sn:itaoka Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市西区の建築物]] [[Category:熊本市西区の歴史]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] {{Commons category|Kitaoka-jinja}} [[ja:北岡神社]] [[sv:Kitaoka-jinja]] [[zh:北冈神社]] {{Commons category|Kitaoka-jinja}} [[ja:北岡神社]] [[sv:Kitaoka-jinja]] [[zh:北冈神社]]

Interchange|qq=}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. *
