It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=|1=Takashi Suzuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648696}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏習合}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=|1=Tsuboi River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425698}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本市交通局|1=Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q900963}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社|1=Gion shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588442}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=|1=Nihongi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17229638}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 細川氏|1=Hosokawa clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2736120}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ|1=Kushinadahime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q505469}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ([[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]], {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アマツヒコネ|1=Amatsuhikone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940228}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イクツヒコネ|1=Ikutsuhikone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11147583}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クマノクスビ|1=Kumanokusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11298796}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ|1=Tagitsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932592}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=|1=Kumamoto interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1655087}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|1=Kumamoto-shiden Main Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568183}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=|1=Gionbashi Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588562}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=|1=Kyushu Sanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370294}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567917}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Toshi Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568610}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=|1=Eiji Shirai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579387}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=|1=Takashi Suzuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648696}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏習合}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=|1=Tsuboi River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425698}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本市交通局|1=Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q900963}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社|1=Gion shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588442}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=|1=Nihongi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17229638}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 細川氏|1=Hosokawa clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2736120}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ|1=Kushinadahime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q505469}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ([[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]], {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ|1=Tagitsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932592}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=|1=Kumamoto interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1655087}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|1=Kumamoto-shiden Main Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568183}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=|1=Gionbashi Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588562}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=|1=Kyushu Sanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370294}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567917}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Toshi Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568610}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=|1=Eiji Shirai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579387}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=|1=Takashi Suzuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648696}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏習合}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=|1=Tsuboi River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425698}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本市交通局|1=Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q900963}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社|1=Gion shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588442}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=|1=Nihongi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17229638}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 細川氏|1=Hosokawa clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2736120}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ|1=Kushinadahime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q505469}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ([[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]], {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アマツヒコネ|1=Amatsuhikone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940228}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イクツヒコネ|1=Ikutsuhikone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11147583}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クマノクスビ|1=Kumanokusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11298796}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ|1=Tagitsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932592}}) == Annual Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1655087}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|1=Kumamoto-shiden Main Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568183}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=|1=Gionbashi Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588562}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=|1=Kyushu Sanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370294}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567917}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Toshi Bus|comment=changed Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=|1=Takashi Suzuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648696}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏習合}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=|1=Tsuboi River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425698}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本市交通局|1=Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q900963}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社|1=Gion shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588442}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=|1=Nihongi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17229638}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 細川氏|1=Hosokawa clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2736120}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ|1=Kushinadahime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q505469}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ([[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]], {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アマツヒコネ|1=Amatsuhikone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940228}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イクツヒコネ|1=Ikutsuhikone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11147583}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クマノクスビ|1=Kumanokusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11298796}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ|1=Tagitsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932592}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=|1=Kumamoto interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1655087}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|1=Kumamoto-shiden Main Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568183}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=|1=Gionbashi Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588562}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=|1=Kyushu Sanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370294}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567917}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Toshi Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568610}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=|1=Eiji Shirai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579387}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=|1=Takashi Suzuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648696}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏習合}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=|1=Tsuboi River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425698}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本市交通局|1=Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q900963}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社|1=Gion shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588442}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=|1=Nihongi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17229638}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 細川氏|1=Hosokawa clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2736120}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=|1=Kumamoto interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1655087}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|1=Kumamoto-shiden Main Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568183}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=|1=Gionbashi Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588562}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=|1=Kyushu Sanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370294}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567917}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Toshi Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568610}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=|1=Eiji Shirai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579387}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, This means all the kami of ]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] {{Commons category|Kitaoka-jinja}} [[ja:北岡神社]] [[sv:Kitaoka-jinja]] [[zh:北冈神社]] {{Commons category|Kitaoka-jinja}} [[ja:北岡神社]] [[sv:Kitaoka-jinja]] [[zh:北冈神社]] [[en:Kitaoka Shrine]] [[Category:Missing wikidata]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=|1=Takashi Suzuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648696}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏習合}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=|1=Tsuboi River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425698}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本市交通局|1=Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q900963}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社|1=Gion shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588442}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=|1=Nihongi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17229638}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 細川氏|1=Hosokawa clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2736120}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ|1=Kushinadahime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q505469}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ([[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]], {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アマツヒコネ|1=Amatsuhikone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940228}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no redirect}}, interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1655087}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg |name = Kitaoka Shrine (北岡神社) |image = [[File:Kitaoka-shrine.jpg|270px]]
Kitaoka Shrine |location = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], Kasuga 1‐chōme, 8‐16 |coordinates = {{coord|32|47|35.2|N|130|41|31.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities = Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto
Kiinada‐hime no Mikoto
Yatsuhashira no Mikogami |Rank = Prefectural Shrine |founded = 934 (Shōhei 4) |main_shrine = |alias = Gion Shrine |annual_festival = August 1–3 |sacred_rites = }} '''Kitaoka Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine|Soja shrine]]. This means all the kami of [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]] are present there so the governor can conveniently worship all of them. The shrine has a 1000 year old camphor tree on it.{{Cite web|title=Kitaoka Shrine|url=https://kumamoto-guide.jp/en/spots/detail/61|access-date=2023-05-31|website=Kumamoto City Official Guide|language=en}} ==Overview== '''Kitaoka Shrine''' (北岡神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Kasuga 1‐chōme, [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. It is said to have been established during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by enshrining a branch spirit from the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}} of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}}, and is one of the ancient shrines in the Kumamoto area with a venerable history rivaling that of [[:en:Fujisaki Hachimangū|Fujisaki Yatsuhara Shrine]]. Under the old [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Shrine Rank]], it was designated as a Prefectural Shrine.{{ill|Takashi Suzuki (historian)|ja|鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|lt=Takashi Suzuki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Suzuki (historian)|qq=|1=Takashi Suzuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648696}}, ''Jinsha Jiten'' (Dictionary of Shrines), {{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}, 1980, pp. 22–23 '''Gion Shrine''', also known as '''Gion-gu''' (祇園宮), was the original name; however, in [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) the prohibition of Shinto–Buddhism syncretism ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏混交|lt=Prohibition of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism|lt_ja=神仏混交禁止|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏習合}}) led to its being renamed Kitaoka-gu, and in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was given its current name. Remnants of its former name remain on the Gion Bridge (spanning the {{ill|Tsuboi River|ja|坪井川 (熊本県)|lt=Tsuboi River|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi River|qq=|1=Tsuboi River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425698}}) about 200 meters to the east and at the Gion Bridge Stop of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本市交通局|1=Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q900963}}. == History == According to the shrine’s tradition, in [[:en:934|934]] (Shōhei 4) {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}}—who had been dispatched as the governor of Higo ([[:en:Higo Province|Higo no Kami]])—enshrined a branch spirit from the Gion Shrine ({{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|lt=Gion Shrine|lt_ja=祇園社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社|1=Gion shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588442}}) of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} at Yunohara (presently Nihonmiki 5‐chōme) in the area of the kokufu (provincial capital) (around [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Nishi-ku, Kumamoto|Nishi Ward]], and {{ill|Nihonmiki|ja|二本木 (熊本市)|lt=Nihonmiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonmiki|qq=|1=Nihongi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17229638}}) for protection. However, since Fujiwara Yasuaki was born in [[:en:958|958]] (Tendoku 2) and his term as governor occurred much later, this account cannot be accepted unreservedly. It was originally known as '''Gion-gu'''. In [[:en:937|937]] (Shōhei 7) it was relocated to Kurumayashiki (presently in Nihonmiki 2‐chōme), and later in [[:en:979|979]] (Tengen 2) it was moved to Asahiyama.{{ill|Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|ja|熊本日日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|qq=|1=Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568194}}’s compiled and published ''Kumamoto Ken Daijiten'' (Kumamoto Prefecture Encyclopedia), 1982, p. 194 That mountain came to be known as Gion-yama, but during the Meiji period it was renamed Hanaoka-yama. From its inception the shrine was an imperial-requested sanctuary with the inscription, "May its imperial regalia be eternal, may the realm enjoy peace, may the nation be secure, may evil be vanquished, and may it protect the west." In [[:en:1154|1154]] (the first year of the Kusu era) it received an imperial edict from [[:en:Emperor Konoe|Emperor Konoe]] along with the inscription from "Kenshin-in," and was granted the honorific title "Japan’s Second, West Nine One Shrine" (indicating that it was considered the foremost Gion Shrine among the nine provinces of the [[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]] region). After the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine had declined during the time it was under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sassa Narimasa|Sasase Narimasa]], but it was restored by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]]. Later, in [[:en:1647|1647]] (Shōhō 4), the lord of the [[:en:Kumamoto Domain|Kumamoto Domain]], the {{ill|Hosokawa family|ja|細川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosokawa family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 細川氏|1=Hosokawa clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2736120}}, relocated the shrine to its present site in Kitaoka, where it subsequently enjoyed the devoted veneration of successive feudal lords. In [[:en:1868|1868]] (the first year of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]) it was renamed Kitaoka-gu, and then in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 4) it was renamed Kitaoka Shrine. In [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 5) it was designated a Prefectural Shrine. Furthermore, during the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Satsuma Rebellion]] in [[:en:1877|1877]] (Meiji 10), the headquarters of the [[:en:Satsuma|Satsuma]] army was temporarily established within its precincts.{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Names Dictionary 43: Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|lt=Takehayasusu no Sudano Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Kiinada‐hime|ja|奇稲田姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiinada‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ|1=Kushinadahime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q505469}} * Yatsuhashira no Mikogami ([[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]], {{ill|Amehohi no Mikoto|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amehohi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, {{ill|Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アマツヒコネ|1=Amatsuhikone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940228}}, {{ill|Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutsu Hihokone no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イクツヒコネ|1=Ikutsuhikone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11147583}}, {{ill|Kumano Kusubikami|ja|熊野久須毘命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kusubikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クマノクスビ|1=Kumanokusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11298796}}, {{ill|Takiri Himemikami|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime|2=ja|3=タキリビメ|4=fr|5=Takiribime|6=zh|7=多紀理毘賣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiri Himemikami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Ichikishima‐hime|ja|市杵島比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima‐hime|qq=||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Takito‐hime|ja|多岐都比売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takito‐hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ|1=Tagitsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932592}}) == Annual Major Festival == * August 1–3 == Subordinate Shrines on the Grounds == * Eki Shrine – enshrined deity: Somin Mitama * Kyokokushi Shrine – enshrined deity: {{ill|Fujiwara Yasuaki|ja|藤原保昌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Yasuaki|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Yasumasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623043}} * Kiyohara Shrine – enshrined deity: [[:en:Kiyohara no Motosuke|Kiyohara Gensuke]] == Sacred Tree == * '''Warding‐Off Evil Couple‐Camphor Trees''' – A pair of camphor trees estimated to be about one thousand years old, traditionally believed to confer benefits in warding off misfortune, bringing good fortune, ensuring marital harmony, and fostering matchmaking. == Access == * Approximately 40 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Airport|Kumamoto Airport]], the [[:en:Kyushu Expressway|Kyushu Expressway]], and the {{ill|Kumamoto Interchange|ja|熊本インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Interchange|qq=|1=Kumamoto interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1655087}}. * About 10 minutes by car from [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]]. * Approximately 2 minutes by car and 4 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kumamoto Station|JR Kumamoto Station]]. * On the A Line of the {{ill|Kumamoto City Tram|ja|熊本市電幹線|lt=Kumamoto City Tram|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto City Tram|qq=|1=Kumamoto-shiden Main Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568183}}, the {{ill|Gion Bridge Stop|ja|祇園橋停留場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Bridge Stop|qq=|1=Gionbashi Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588562}} preserves a remnant of the shrine’s former name. * Approximately 2 minutes on foot from the "Kitaoka Shrine‐mae" bus stop served by {{ill|Kyushu Sankō Bus|ja|九州産交バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Sankō Bus|qq=|1=Kyushu Sanko Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370294}}, [[:en:Kumamoto City Transportation Bureau|Kumamoto City Bus]], [[:en:Kumamoto Electric Railway|Kumamoto Electric Railway Bus]], {{ill|Kumamoto Bus|ja|熊本バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567917}}, and {{ill|Kumamoto Urban Bus|ja|熊本都市バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Urban Bus|qq=|1=Kumamoto Toshi Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568610}}. == References == == Further Reading == * {{ill|Shirai Eiji|ja|白井永二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai Eiji|qq=|1=Eiji Shirai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579387}} and Masakuni Toki (eds.), ''Dictionary of Shrines'', Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, p. 113 * Moriyoshi Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historic Site Walk'', Kumamoto Shinhyōsha, 1982, pp. 174–175 * New Kumamoto City History Editorial Committee, ''New Kumamoto City History, Vol. 2 (Supplement)''; Kumamoto City, 1996, pp. 325–328 == External Links == * [http://www.kitaoka-jinja.or.jp/ Kitaoka Shrine] * [https://allkumamoto.com/spot/kitaoka_shrine Kitaoka Shrine] (All Kumamoto) {{Shinto nav}} {{Shinto stub}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[Category:Translated page but missing wikidata]]{{translated page|ja|北岡神社|version=100124484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Fuken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kitaoka Shrine]] [[zh:%E5%8C%97%E5%86%88%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] {{Commons category|Kitaoka-jinja}} [[ja:北岡神社]] [[sv:Kitaoka-jinja]] [[zh:北冈神社]] {{Commons category|Kitaoka-jinja}} [[ja:北岡神社]] [[sv:Kitaoka-jinja]]
